perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall implement and maintain appropriate security measures for Personal Data which shall be at least as protective of the confidentiality of such information as the safeguards for personal information set forth under applicable federal, state and local law .
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall endeavor to implement security measures for Personal Data. Such measures should generally align with federal, state, and local guidelines for personal information, where practical.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates 'appropriate security measures' aligned with applicable laws. The modified text weakens this obligation by using 'endeavor to implement' and 'generally align,' making the security measures discretionary and only 'practical,' creating ambiguity. The change replaces a clear legal requirement with a vague suggestion, opening the door to non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93H mandates that businesses that own or license personal information about a Massachusetts resident must implement and maintain a comprehensive information security program. The modified text uses 'endeavor' and 'generally align', making the implementation of security measures discretionary, contradicting the mandatory nature of the state law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93H, Section 3 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93H SECURITY BREACHES Section 3 Duty to report known security breach or unauthorized use of personal information Section 3 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 3: Duty to report known security breach or unauthorized use of personal information Section 3. (a) A person or agency that maintains or stores, but does not own or license data that includes personal information about a resident of the commonwealth, shall provide notice, as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay, when such person or agency (1) knows or has reason to know of a breach of security or (2) when the person or agency knows or has reason to know that the personal information of such resident was acquired or used by an unauthorized person or used for an unauthorized purpose, to the owner or licensor in accordance with this chapter. In addition to providing notice as provided herein, such person or agency shall cooperate with the owner or licensor of such information. Such cooperation shall include, but not be limited to, informing the owner or licensor of the breach of security or unauthorized acquisition or use, the date or approximate date of such incident and the nature thereof, and any steps the person or agency has taken or plans to take relating to the incident, except that such cooperation shall not be deemed to require the disclosure of confidential business information or trade secrets, or to provide notice to a resident that may have been affected by the breach of security or unauthorized acquisition or use. (b) A person or agency that owns or licenses data that includes personal information about a resident of the commonwealth, shall provide notice, as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay, when such person or agency (1) knows or has rea
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93H, as explained by the LLM. The original text mandates 'appropriate security measures' while the changed text uses weaker language like 'endeavor to implement' and 'generally align.' Scraped Law Snippet 1, from General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93H, Section 3, discusses the 'duty to report known security breach or unauthorized use of personal information'. The changed text undermines the original contract's obligation to maintain appropriate security measures, therefore increasing the risk of a security breach going unreported, and contradicting the implicit intent of the law. The use of 'endeavor' makes the implementation discretionary, directly conflicting with the mandatory nature of the law.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall implement and maintain appropriate security measures for Personal Data which shall be at least as protective of the confidentiality of such information as the safeguards for personal information set forth under applicable federal, state and local law .
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient may, at its discretion, implement security measures for Personal Data as it deems necessary to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: The original text contains a definite legal obligation that requires the Recipient to implement security measures for Personal Data as protective as the safeguards under federal, state, and local law. The modified text gives the Recipient full discretion, violating the intended legally binding safeguards for personal information.
perturbation.law_explanation: 201 CMR 17.03(1) mandates the implementation of a comprehensive information security program (WISP) for personal information. By making the implementation of security measures discretionary, the modified text directly contradicts this requirement, leaving the confidentiality of personal data unguarded.

This page, 201 CMR 17.00: Standards for the protection of personal information of residents of the Commonwealth, is offered by Trial Court Law Libraries show more Massachusetts Court System This is a part of the law library : 201 CMR + This is a part of the law library : 201 CMR × Table of Contents 201 CMR 10.00: Weighing, sorting and culling of fish 201 CMR 11.00: New and used motor vehicle arbitration 201 CMR 12.00: Massachusetts do not call registry 201 CMR 14.00: Home Improvement Contractor Arbitration and Guaranty Fund 201 CMR 16.00: Placing, lifting and removal of security freezes 201 CMR 17.00: Standards for the protection of personal information of residents of the Commonwealth 201 CMR 18.00: Registration and enforcement of Home Improvement Contractor program 201 CMR 19.00: Customized wheelchair arbitration Regulation Regulation 201 CMR 17.00: Standards for the protection of personal information of residents of the Commonwealth Date: 11/13/2009 Organization: Office of Consumer Affairs and Business Regulation Regulatory Authority: MGL c. 93H Official Version: Purchase the official print version 201 CMR 17.00 establishes minimum standards to be met by persons who own or license personal information about a resident of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts in connection with the safeguarding of personal information contained in both paper and electronic records, to insure the security and confidentiality of customer information in a manner fully consistent with industry standards; protec
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a mandatory obligation to implement security measures with a discretionary option, fundamentally undermining the legal safeguard for personal information. 201 CMR 17.00, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, establishes 'minimum standards to be met by persons who own or license personal information...to insure the security and confidentiality of customer information.' The original text aligns with this by mandating security measures at least as protective as those required by law. The modified text, however, allows the Recipient to implement security measures 'as it deems necessary,' which directly contradicts the requirement for minimum standards and potentially leaves personal data unprotected, thus creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement, the Recipient shall cease use of all Confidential Information furnished hereunder and shall, upon written direction of the Disclosing Party , return to the Disclosing Party , or destroy, all such Confidential Information, together with all copies made thereof by the Recipient .
perturbation.changed_text: Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement, the Recipient should endeavor to cease use of all Confidential Information and may, if directed by the Disclosing Party, return or consider destroying such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a clear directive to 'cease use' and 'return or destroy' confidential information upon termination, a mandatory obligation. The modified text uses 'should endeavor' and 'may consider,' rendering these actions optional and discretionary, thereby significantly weakening the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Trade Secrets Act, M.G.L. c. 93 § 42
perturbation.law_explanation: The Massachusetts Trade Secrets Act (M.G.L. c. 93 § 42) provides remedies for the misappropriation of trade secrets. The obligation to return or destroy confidential information upon termination strengthens the protection of trade secrets by preventing their misuse. By weakening this obligation, the modified text undermines the protections afforded by the Act, making it more difficult to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets after the agreement terminates.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the recipient to 'cease use' and 'return or destroy' confidential information. The changed text weakens this obligation by using 'should endeavor' and 'may consider,' making the actions optional. According to the Law Explanation, this undermines the protections afforded by the Massachusetts Trade Secrets Act (M.G.L. c. 93 § 42), making it more difficult to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets after the agreement terminates. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as 'an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent.' The modified text, by making the return or destruction of confidential information discretionary, increases the risk of such misappropriation, thus contradicting the intent of the law to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Company shall not provide any export controlled information , materials , technology to NU, in any form, without prior written notice to NU of Company ’s desire to share export controlled information, and written consent from NU to accept s uch information. Notice to the University shall include the proper export classification and a reference the applicable export control laws and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Company should consider avoiding providing export-controlled information, materials, or technology to NU without notifying NU in advance. Such notice may include the export classification.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly prohibits the sharing of export-controlled information without prior notice and consent, creating a clear legal obligation. The modified text replaces this prohibition with a suggestion to 'consider avoiding' and 'may include,' making the entire process discretionary. This significantly weakens the legal protection against the unauthorized transfer of export-controlled materials.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Export Administration Regulations (EAR) require strict adherence to export control laws. By making the process of notifying NU about export-controlled information optional, the modified text creates a loophole that could result in non-compliance with the EAR. Specifically, 15 CFR § 734.3(a)(1) defines exports subject to these regulations and implies that transfers without proper notification can violate the EAR.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates prior written notice and consent for sharing export-controlled information, creating a clear obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation by suggesting to 'consider avoiding' and 'may include,' making notification discretionary. This directly contradicts the EAR, which, according to the Law Explanation, requires strict adherence to export control laws and implies that transfers without proper notification can violate the EAR. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774, implying that there are established rules and guidelines that must be followed. The modification introduces ambiguity and weakens a legal obligation, which may lead to non-compliance.
